item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-003 is the prime example of what rewrites should be.

The Review
+1

One of the unique things about the standard SCP format is how the containment procedures can be used to play with the reader’s perception of the anomaly. Honestly, any time I reread this scip, I always get confused when reading the containment procedures because I remember the item as being something good (my confusion is a GOOD thing).
By reading past comments, it’s apparent that there have been some issues with the use of withheld information, mainly in abundance. However, the article currently presents the idea of a biological motherboard in a clear way for those that understand the purpose of a motherboard. For clarification, a motherboard connects all the pieces of a computer together. As such, the idea that it grows makes technical sense, as things such as the central processor mentioned need room to fit. My only criticism would probably be a lack of more technical terminology. I want to know what other parts look like, where is the memory, what does the storage look like? Otherwise, a pretty solid concept.
The narrative side of this scip ties everything up with a nice little bow. We realize that we are dealing with an unreliable narrator, but are still left with enough questions that we thirst for more information about this object, particularly its origins. A very good use of narrative without bogging down the concept itself.

+1

I wish we saw more rewrites like this. Passionate authors who aren’t afraid to push the limits of a an accepted concept. Hats off to Moose!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC BY 3.0
Title: Microchip-24lcs52-cp-HD.jpg
Author: ZeptoBars
Release year: 2014

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It felt a bit random? What is the point of it being a motherboard? Maybe it was programmed to grow like that, like the motherboard is DNA or a brain to the creature? I don’t know about this one tbh, but if it is what I guessed, then that’s an awesome idea.
Either way, anomalous and mysterious enough for me.

+1

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Belated notice.

CC-BY-NC is not compatible with CC-BY-SA. The image has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-003-1 appears to be composed of chitin, hair, and nails of unknown biology

One of our readers noted that hair and nails are way more specific than chitin, and usually belong to a given species. The fix he proposed is along the lines of:

appears to be composed of a compound of chitin with materials similar in quality to hair and nails (keratin-protein compounds, zinc, chrome, phosphorus, selenium and calcium)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing concept and article. I don't think it would be inappropriate to refer to this article as being ahead of its time (considering the amount of detail put into this article with it also being the 3rd SCP in series I).
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The biological computer, also known as one of my favourite scips. An anomaly capable of restructuring the world carries lot of weight, and upon reading the whole text, it becomes brutally clear why we need to follow it’s procedures.
This entry can be split into two parts, the first being the description, the second, the interview. The first feels a bit longwinded, having to describe two objects rather than one with the first addendum feeling entirely unnecessary, but the whole thing shines on reaching the second bit, which brings the whole scip together. We know what it does, how it does it, and it’s awe-inspiring.
I do have a few criticisms, such as the first addendum, the fact that O5-10s motive isn’t really hinted on, or that he appears as one of the o-fives at the interview (why is he there? Didn’t he get discharged? Is this the new ten?) and the fact that the fact that it’s a biological computer didn’t really add anything, but the genius idea and great execution overshadow it all.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel so bad for her :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was obviously made by DAZ BUGFEWTZ.
But seriously, who else could it be. I mean, it's thousands of years old, biological technology (which the Bigfoots (Bigfeet? (Woah, double TRIPLE parentheses, all the way)) are famous for using) and has some weird telepathic effect, like some BF tech.
So, it might be a good idea to crosslink towards that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Shona, I’d love a fully-fleshed out tale exploring a world where SCP-003’s ‘paradise’ has come true.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm always intrigued by creatures and entities that wish to perfect our world and our existences simply for our sakes, whether out of a weird stain of empathy and sense of duty or 'systems are supposed to function perfectly'. I'm curious as to what 003's version of perfect paradise is. Copies of her? A playground for us that has been enveloped by her? So many questions.

item.title: Re: +300!
item.content:encoded:
Kudos, Doctor Mann (and various editors)

Mann wrote exactly none of this article. I rewrote it based on a deleted original. I will always hate him for posting it instead of me. ;)

item.title: Amazing!
item.content:encoded: One of the finer Series I contents. My only question is… Where is it stored? Is it in site 19, or any other redacted location? That's the only info missing.

Kudos, Doctor Mann (and various editors); This was a really good one.

honestly; if I were to say anything about, I'd think an increase in temperature would be a more ideal trigger (an increase of energy supply), but what do I know? I'm new.

Again, Good One.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The transcript of the interview was very intriguing and interesting, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn almost sounds like my rehash of the idea for the warden…. all is needed is for her to want to love you and be all motherly. Only the warden isn't strong enough to spread over the world.

I would suggest adding a note to have some one on hand at all times who has passed a series of psychological profiles set aside in "procedure 003-##" or the like. That way the risk of a containment breach not being resolved and SPC-003 basing it's 'paradise' of something wholly awful are reduced.

Just think if some one tossing D-class personal in there and then SCP-003 starting to rebuild the world to suit the paradise of a cereal killer….. nope nope nope… best have some one in there with some sensible ideas….

Perhaps some one brainwashed into thinking a certain way. >.>

I can't say I'm not impressed, despite the weirdly-phrased talking-to at the end. From what I can tell, this is one of the first 'does something vague and then you know what it does because there's a convenient note' skips, which I like probably a little more than I should.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Looks good. Thanks moose!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Added the following paragraph, subject to change/tweaks:

SCP-003-1's biology has been the subject of extensive study. Significant elements have been identified similar to SCP-███, SCP-1512, and SCP-2756, the latter two of which have no further confirmed connection with SCP-003-1 and no known connection each other, and both of which are also not fully understood (technically, even less so than SCP-003, thanks to the extensive cross-disciplinary research on the SCP-003 objects). To date, no convincing analysis has been put forward which satisfactorily explains SCP-003-1's connection to these SCP objects or others, nor its connection to modern technology beyond appearance (and potential mimicry via unknown mechanism).

I moved the suggested edit from the first line because I think that a 'second sentence' edit like that only works if the objects are either redacted or deeply connected, and these are obviously post-hoc additions :) (if I used that word right!)

This paragraph is at the end of the Description.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Delayed response!

I don't enjoy 1865 so much, but 2756 seems like a good choice. Thanks for the suggestion! Adding 2756, and open to other suggestions.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: It's a good idea, but Broken God and Flesh That Hates are already super popular and part of Series 1, and therefore I'm not sure how much mileage there is to be gotten from fusing (heh) the narratives. Our meta-goal for Series 1 SCPs is to connect them to non-Series 1 stuff.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I know it's another author's baby, and this might be the 2 AM talking, but this begs for one link to be Broken God related, and another to be Flesh That Hates related, implying that somebody tried to fuse the two to create utopia.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Something with opaque motivation/nature from Series II or III? You're a tough customer, aren't you :P

Try SCP-1865 or SCP-2756 on for size.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Ah, see I misunderstood the comparison being made in that sentence. I'll see what can be found in the weird thing we don't understand line.

Edit: What about SCP-1512. That definitely fits with the weird things we don't understand call to arms in that its a lovecraftian eldritch entity the foundation is still largely baffled by.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I'm only half awake so will probably have more to say later, but — this is the wrong choice of crosslink there. The SCPs in that sentence should definitely not be the same type of Thing as SCP-003 — they shouldn't be biological things that look like modern machinery. (Also, that's using meta/writer logic, not in-universe logic.)

SCPs to go in that sentence ought to be really fucking weird things, whether eldritch or mundane, things that people really don't understand at all. By 'understand' I mean 'don't get the motivation or nature of' — SCP-1850 is a great suggestion below, but it's really very much something we can understand — it's only the process of creation that's baffling.

So I wouldn't want to use this suggested edit, but an alternate might work. I do want this SCP crosslinked, maybe with multiple crosslinks, if that can work creatively. Willing to edit the SCP as well to add more room — but I don't really know which crosslinks could be added, so more suggestions would be extremely welcome.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: SCP-127 and SCP-1850 are some other biomechanical thingies.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired. Edits made by author. See discussion below.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

This is a very well done skip that really sets a high bar for the bio-tech hybrid articles on the wiki. With a line like "SCP-003-1 appears to be composed of chitin, hair, and nails of unknown biology similar to SCP-████ and SCP-████, arranged in a configuration similar to that of a computer motherboard," one would think that this article encourages the existence of at least two other SCP objects that are similar. Such an article does exist in SCP-2535. As such, I am proposing the following edit:

Changing the following:

"SCP-003-1 appears to be composed of chitin, hair, and nails of unknown biology similar to SCP-████ and SCP-████, arranged in a configuration similar to that of a computer motherboard."

To this:

"SCP-003-1 appears to be composed of chitin, hair, and nails of unknown biology similar to SCP-2535 and SCP-████, arranged in a configuration similar to that of a computer motherboard.

Its a pretty simple in-line link, that uses an already existing comparison that has been written into the document. Doesn't come less disruptive than that.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Beautifully crafted
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP. The thought that an alien life form exists, and is attempting to connect with other living organisms is astounding. It rings similar to the movie The Abyss. But this is much more mysterious and powerful.

A living computer, designed by another species to create paradise like worlds specific to the life form it comes into contact with. And that paradise can be affected based on the biology and psychology of the life form.

003 is so strange too!!! It's a wonderfully deep creation that exists inside of the imagination. And it never seems the same to each person that reads this! Just reading this makes me think so many things. How did it come to be on our planet? Was it put here? Was it an accident left behind? Did it make the world the way it is now or is it here to change it? I have so many questions flowing in my mind I want to explore and think about with this SCP!!!!

This gets an upvote from me. I love this!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HERETIC!!!!!!

Actually, it's funny you say that. I had in my only entry, SCP-1139, that the church of the broken god called 1139 the "Tongue". Interesting cross promotional head cannon. Is this idea worth a story maybe?

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded: The image in this article is a CC BY-NC-SA image from http://www.freefoto.com/preview/04-34-19/Computer-Motherboard. Note that the image is used here under a different license than the rest of the site. Commercial use of the image is not permited by its creator.

item.title: Re: Moose?
item.content:encoded:
I would love to know where this was discovered, and in what condition.

+1, the edit was great.

e: I didn't mean to reply :P

item.title: Moose?
item.content:encoded:
Chalk it up to me being a quiet member of one year, a reader for two, to whatever you like- I'm with deadlymoose on this one.

I've never read this SCP until today; I've read many of tdm's comments in the last couple of years, and the avatar did not stick out at all until I read the discussion here. Even once it was pointed out, Dr. Moose felt like she fit well- she added to the main plot, and did so only by reporting what she experienced. No "wait, I know how to fix it/talk to it/kill it/etc."

I fail to see how this insertion hurt the narrative flow at all, and have long loved reading scips partly because of consistencies like Dr. Wondertainment, the Mobile Task Forces, and Dr. Bright, to name a few.

I know there will never be a full universe (there almost can't be), but some level of world building is necessary to pull a reader in. Without a consistent world for all of the scips and tales to live in (however simplified and loose), the whole site would be very disjointed and something much less interesting.

Before I go completely off-topic: I like 003 very much. As a longtime Trekkie and Bradbury fan, the 'benevolent agency that wreaks incredible harm' is still a source of spine-tingling nostalgia. Plus, you left the amount of questions as to "Why does it?" balanced quite nicely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An outstanding rewrite of a classic article, nothing but commendable and truly deserving of

Interviewed: Dr. Tilda David Moose

Downvoted. This is tacky, unnecessary, extremely obvious and easily outweighs any positives the article has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Headcanon time:

This is the Soul of the Broken God. 882 is the Heart. 914 is the Mind. 808 is the Voice. 217-infected people are its Hands. It would NOT be a bad thing for it to be rebuilt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really, really happy about your reaction. :D

Since it was the material I came up with in friggin' 2011, I was seriously worried it wouldn't hold up once actually revealed; it was mainly TwistedGears' reaction to me spilling all the info in a PM ('wait, that's way more interesting than what you have now') that convinced me to un-expunge it.

And I love the questions you asked. Those are some of the questions I hoped the reader would ask!

(An aside by way of non-answering: The article would seem to say fuck no, but why then did the former O5-10 try to make it happen anyway? Did he have a good reason? There's plenty of possible explanation, but I was trying to make the reader want to ask. I originally had Tilda speculate, but it didn't work, so I cut it.)

Also, I'll fix those two nitpicks immediately. :) (It's definitely recovered from — typo on my part. And yes, it should be 'emit' — another typo.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally got around to reading this. For context, I haven't read the pre-2011 version, and was conflicted on the 2011 version, alternatively downvoting or neutral-voting it. It had some nice bits that I liked, but the expungement and some other things related to/caused by the expungement seriously ruined it for me.

I expected the rewrite to touch up the tone perhaps, unexpunge some stuff, and make the article good enough for a hesitant upvote, but not enough to make me really like the article. The stuff above the collapsible is pretty much what I expected.

The interview is amazing. Not because it's particularly brilliantly written or anything, but because it finally makes the whole thing fit together. It gives the skip a clear agency, which is delightfully warped. Before, to me, it was just a collection of random bits and pieces, together for no clear reason, some of which happened to be interesting. Now, I understand what's going on, and it's a whole cohesive story, even if I don't know all the pieces of it. And the pieces I still miss now make me ask interesting questions, instead of just sighing in annoyance and moving on. Like, were the creators of 003-2 malicious, purposefully denying humanity the paradise, not because they were afraid of being converted, but because they thought humanity didn't deserve one?

This is still not in my top-favorite articles on the site, but I like it a lot now.

► A couple of nitpicks now
▼ Two nitpicky nitpicks

The runes on SCP-003-2 are not part of any known language, and emits pale, flickering light patterns.

Should this be "emit"? If it is 003-2 that emits them and not the runes, then this sentence is just broken.

Partially interpreted data recovered SCP-003-2 may

Could be either "recovered with" or "recovered from", which seems like a significant enough difference to not just fix it myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. That was done for readability; doesn't technically count as un-expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only complaint is that a whole lot of the "un-expungements" just seem to be replacing one non-answer ("[DATA EXPUNGED]") with another non-answer ("through anomalous means").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be like me suddenly rewriting 1658, my highest rated SCP, into a self-insert fanfic about Dr. Issac H. Pickman and how he went back in time to save the Library of Alexandria with help from the Super-Mold People.

No it wouldn't. It would be like you un-expunging the name of Dr. Issac H. Pickman.

If you would like to do this, absolutely go for it. I see no reason why you should not. (Though — others would, as you see.)

In fact, the fact that this was rewritten at all is entirely suspect in my eyes. Where are the records of the approval of this rewrite? There's nothing on the thread itself, and there doesn't appear to be anything on O5.

I thought it was fairly clear from this comment thread, despite that the records were accidentally deleted, that I rewrote this in 2011 after it was archived/deleted (that part might not be clear — both actually happened), and received unanimous administrative approval for the total rewrite. I have had ownership of this article since, because the vast majority of the material is mine.

What I did just now was un-expunge almost everything… which included a name of my author avatar. It's only one line out of a fairly major update, but controversial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I may give input: the fact that the author avatar was added in the rewrite of an article rated above +100 smacks of "I can do anything I want because this will never be deleted". That would be like me suddenly rewriting 1658, my highest rated SCP, into a self-insert fanfic about Dr. Issac H. Pickman and how he went back in time to save the Library of Alexandria with help from the Super-Mold People.

In fact, the fact that this was rewritten at all is entirely suspect in my eyes. Where are the records of the approval of this rewrite? There's nothing on the thread itself, and there doesn't appear to be anything on O5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"tee hee isn't my OC cool?"

It does not imply this. It implies nothing at all beyond that it is an author avatar.

This is not a well developed character that I have grown to know and understand across a series of tales.

This is true. However, if I were to characterize this person in this article, it would derail from the point of the article. It would be an artistic error. Not that you are suggesting this, mind you; I'm explaining in full for clarity's sake. and because I enjoy making people suffer through walls of text

The necessity of building characters up in tales only holds true if there was something to be ashamed about re: author avatars; if the old author avatars are exclusively awful without special justification.

I see it as fair to hold that stance. However, I do not agree with that stance.

Further arguments in favor of author avatars
Hide

it encourages others to make likewise boring self-inserts to throw in their articles

Quibble: Not boring. It just is. It merely exists.

To be clear, I don't mind if people make self-inserts undeveloped beyond a name, so long as the names aren't worse than Gears or Moose or Lament or Breen or whatnot.

This is already done, mind you. I'd not even draw attention to this instance except that there's a debate associated with it and a point I wish to make — and, well, because I am an admin and it's 003, there is no choice but to have said debate.

If the avatar does stuff in the SCP article that is actively boring or stupid, or the stories themselves are bland and boring (or shitty), then that is something I do not support.

But simply existing isn't an issue for me.

I do intend to develop this character, but the article content doesn't require that. It stands on its own. It is a mere setting detail, like referencing use of remote viewing without explaining how that works.

Finally, I argue that name-dropping researchers can cause readers to react with interest. They can see it as adding more depth. Hence why people get so obsessed with characters in the first place, even when their first encounter with those characters shows them doing essentially nothing. (I should call that the Iceberg principle: a character who never did jackshit but everyone remembers due to the name-dropping.) Therefore, character name-dropping can act as a hook, and indication of a wider world.

And if I suddenly fuck off and don't write any more material… I still think the principle of the thing outweighs the detriment to a subset of readers.

e: The proceeding argument only really makes sense from a perspective of "I hate this shit already". I am nothing if not biased as all hell.

Fair enough. Pretty much all your argument does make sense from this perspective.

Hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I too will reply for the audience's sake.

When used like this, in an article, the only content of that character is the name (and yes, that name is hella snowflaky, you should change it). This is not a well developed character that I have grown to know and understand across a series of tales. It is a name alone. This then makes the self-insert not only tacky, but boring. It lacks any sort of positive quality or purpose beyond "tee hee isn't my OC cool?" And this ruins the article for me. While I do not support self-inserts, under certain circumstances I can forgive them and look the other way. If we are to have them, I would like to see more Kate McTiriss-es, because Kate McTiriss is a character who has been fleshed out, and is well-written and interesting. This is not Kate McTiriss-like.

Boring namedrops do not solve the problem presented: If anything, it encourages others to make likewise boring featureless self-inserts to throw in their articles, and for this I will not stand.

e: The proceeding argument only really makes sense from a perspective of "I hate this shit already". I am nothing if not biased as all hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We already know each others' thoughts on this, but I'd like to reply on here for the sake of other readers.

I know that using an author avatar sharing a name with your username is considered tacky by a few significant writers, such as yourself.

However, I feel that ship has already sailed — in 2008.

An aside on the avatar's name
Close aside on the avatar's name

I also know that Tilda David Moose is — while realistic (the surname Moose is surprisingly common) — still a fairly unusual name, with echoes of 'special snowflake'. So I did seriously consider changing the name.

But ultimately, that's how the old style of names like Dr. Gears and Dr. Clef are, already. And I think having continuity with them while adhering to modern quality standards is a net plus. You can't put the genie of "Foundation researchers have unusual names/code-names" back in the box.

And I believe besides the name, my writing of the character's voice has no serious issues. (Note that I am happy to change that monologue if people think it needs work.)

Close aside on the avatar's name

The fact is, we will always be identified by author avatars. At this point, that is impossible to change.

We tried to crack down on them five damn years ago, and we've only existed for seven! And yet the fanbase retains its laser focus on the background material created in those first two years, even as newer SCPs become more and more popular. This has legs.

Because of this, if author avatars are only associated with 2008-2010 work, that is a huge negative for the rest of our fiction, because it promotes the idea that most of the worldbuilding of the Foundationverse became a closed book in 2010 and now only has a few modern SCPs tacked on top.

Plus, most people, even those who love the new stuff, love the avatars. Given the choice, they choose to believe in a Foundation world with the avatars. Because they're a lot of fun. There are exceptions (certain sections of the fanbase loathe them), but still it's undeniable.

I am not saying everything needs author avatars. I am saying I believe using author avatars sometimes, held to current quality standards, and not favoring them over non-self-inserts, is a net benefit. But this also requires the creation and usage of new avatars.

This won't convince you if you hate the old stuff, or if you hate the usage of author avatars. That's reasonable.

However, most people love the old stuff, and enjoy usages of author avatars. People enjoy writing them, despite their pitfalls, because they're fun for most people. I am included in all these categories. Therefore, I am taking the risk of going with what I enjoy.

And we still have modern quality standards. Yes, a few crap avatars will likely slip through, but that isn't really a net change in the situation we already have. So I'm okay with that risk.

item.title: Re: UPDATE
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for this. Now I feel like I have a MUCH better understanding of what this entity is trying to do. But at the same time, many questions are still left unanswered, enough for this to still have the perfect amount of mystery surrounding it.

Also as I was saying in chat: I really don't mind the self-insert as I feel it was tastefully done rather than being some kind of Mary-Sue thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tilda David Moose

No.

This is tacky enough to tear me completely out of the article, and I do not support its inclusion.

item.title: UPDATE
item.content:encoded:
This SCP has now been updated, after repeated requests over the years.

Changes:

  • Cleanup for tone & clarity.
  • Improved containment procedures.
  • Un-expunged almost everything.

Thanks in particular to TwistedGears, TroyL, Soulless, Photosynthetic, and… everyone else I am forgetting offhand who took a crack at this in chat.

A word on expungement: I'm not of the school who thinks pretty much all expungement is bad. But in this specific case, reader reaction was pretty consistent that this would be improved if most of the material was no longer left hidden or barely implied.

And I think they were right, so I've acted on it. It's a pretty big update to an SCP with a rating over 100, but I think it's worth the risk.

(There's actually still a good amount of material left hidden, but it's now more like 10%-20% rather than 50%.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept of an organic computer is pretty cool. Seriously, how neat would it be to have a completely biodegradable computer?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it copy the life form that it comes in contact with or is the humanoid its default form?

The former. What's meant to be implied (successfully or not) is that 003 will try to … 'take into account' whatever sufficiently large organism comes into contact with her/it, and pattern her/its form after this organism during her/its emergence from chrysalis, and probably the resulting restructuring scenario as well.

Bacteria are too small, but a housefly would count… which could end up being very bad for humanity.

What would have happened if it was (for example) a dog that the Object touched?

Then get ready for the Dogscape. (Maybe. Maybe something totally different. But she/it would look like a dog in her/its second stage.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's actually not that much older. If you think the tone is wobbly, please, do point out where that is.

I don't see any tone issues myself, but as I am the writer, it's certainly believable that I'm just too close to the material to see what's right under my nose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-vote. While I like the idea, the tone is wobbly. I can partly forgive that with it being an older scip, but it still could use some dusting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if it's the way it's written or if I am just tired and slow at the moment, but I'm having trouble fully understanding this one. Way too much redaction and expungement is making it tricky to wrap my head around it. It's a circuit board brain that grows a body around it? Does it copy the life form that it comes in contact with or is the humanoid its default form? What would have happened if it was (for example) a dog that the Object touched?

Maybe I misread something or even missed something entirely… I'm gonna go with a neutral vote until I reread it a couple times.

item.title: Re: First impression
item.content:encoded: I remain proud of this comment, and you can't take that away from me.

item.title: Re: First impression
item.content:encoded: augh

item.title: Re: First impression
item.content:encoded: Best…comment…ever!

item.title: First impression
item.content:encoded:
When I first read the containment procedures about the temperature, a really strange scene came to mind:

+ Power Failure
- 'Nuf o' 'dat

Agent S: My God, the power's out. Everywhere. You know what I have to do.

Junior Researcher W: You're a brave man, Smith. The Foundation owes you a debt of graditude. Hurry, now, it's nearly below 35 Celsius.

Agent S: [takes out a straight razor] Tell the Senior Researcher… that while this probably won't kill me, I'm gonna need a few days off. [cuts open the base of his ribcage, slides SCP-003 (which I imagined to be a 4 in. square computer chip) into his chest cavity]

Junior Researcher W: Well done, Smith. That was good work. Have a trophy.

Senior Researcher Bright: Wow the Smith, have a trophy.

Senior Researcher Clef: Here the Smith. Have a trophy.

Everyone: Three trophies for the Smith!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you to the both of you for that. Dunno how I missed that for… forever. :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What are you talking about, those are clearly meters

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there any reason why all the measurements are in feet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been around only for a few months, but this is how I'd imagine the "old foundation". Several ideas in one SCP (PC component, made out of living stuff, reforming to some kind of a medusa (?)), A LOT of expungements, and one of the first numbers in the list. :) The only thing missing is namedropping Clef and Bright.

No vote, I would really appreciate a bit more revealed + I liked the idea and the 003-A and B synergy (or, in this case, the opposite. I mean, how those two things work together. Yep)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, the one thing I would critique against is that there are a couple of unnecessary [REDACTED]'s, especially in the details of the generator and radiation. I mean, shouldn't the Foundation members who are in charge of this thing know what generator to use and how to create similar radiation to stop the thing from growing?

The idea is that people in charge of 003 know, but no one else is allowed to know, because there's something in the hidden details that might cause certain people with access to SCP documentation to try to take some sort of action regarding 003 that the Foundation considers unacceptable. After all, something motivated the O5 to do what they did.

You're right about the generator - but anyone can access and operate Generator 003-IX in case of containment breach, they're just not allowed to know how it works or what it is besides a generator. (Probably powered by the soul of a forsaken child.)
